quiz.title: Unit 6 Quiz 2, Grade 11 Chemistry
quiz.subtitle: Reaction Types and Energy Changes
quiz.subject: Chemistry
quiz.topic: Synthesis, Decomposition, Single Replacement, Double Replacement, Combustion, Exothermic vs Endothermic Reactions, Balancing Equations, Precipitates, Catalysts

questions.question: In the reaction: $\text{Fe} + \text{CuSO}_4 \rightarrow \text{FeSO}_4 + \text{Cu}$, the reaction type is:
questions.explanation: This is a single replacement reaction. Iron replaces copper in copper sulfate to form iron sulfate and copper metal. Iron is more reactive than copper.

questions.question: What type of reaction is: $\text{CaCO}_3 \rightarrow \text{CaO} + \text{CO}_2$?
questions.explanation: This is a decomposition reaction. Calcium carbonate breaks down into calcium oxide and carbon dioxide when heated. A single compound breaks into two or more simpler substances.

questions.question: When magnesium burns in air, it reacts with oxygen to form magnesium oxide. This reaction is:
questions.explanation: Burning magnesium is an exothermic reaction. It releases energy in the form of heat and light, producing a brilliant white flame and forming magnesium oxide.

questions.question: Which is a correct example of a synthesis reaction?
questions.explanation: This is a synthesis reaction where two or more reactants (hydrogen and oxygen) combine to form a single product (water).

questions.question: Which is an example of a decomposition reaction?
questions.explanation: This is a decomposition reaction where a single compound (sodium chloride) breaks down into its elements (sodium and chlorine). Note that this reaction requires electrolysis to occur.

questions.question: Which observation suggests an endothermic reaction?
options: Chemical reaction happens faster than usual
Chemical reaction happens slower than usual
questions.explanation: In an endothermic reaction, energy is absorbed from the surroundings, causing the temperature of the surroundings to decrease. Exothermic reactions cause the temperature to increase.

questions.question: Which is balanced?
questions.explanation: $2\text{H}_2 + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$ is balanced: left side has 4 H and 2 O, right side has 4 H and 2 O. The other options have unequal numbers of atoms.

questions.question: A precipitate is:
questions.explanation: A precipitate is an insoluble solid that forms when two solutions are mixed. It appears as a cloudy or solid material that settles out of the solution.

questions.question: Synthesis reaction: A + B →
questions.explanation: The general form of a synthesis reaction is A + B → AB, where two or more reactants combine to form a single product.

questions.question: Example of decomposition:
questions.explanation: This is a decomposition reaction where calcium carbonate breaks down into calcium oxide and carbon dioxide. A single compound breaks into simpler substances.

questions.question: Which of the following chemical equations is unbalanced?
questions.explanation: Equation C is unbalanced. On the left side, $2\text{KClO}_3$ contains 6 oxygen atoms ($2 \times 3 = 6$). On the right side, $\text{O}_2$ represents only 2 oxygen atoms. To balance it, the equation should be $2\text{KClO}_3 \rightarrow 2\text{KCl} + 3\text{O}_2$. All other equations are properly balanced.

questions.question: Single replacement (displacement): A + BC →
questions.explanation: The general form of a single replacement reaction is A + BC → AC + B, where element A replaces element B in compound BC.

questions.question: In single replacement, metals replace:
questions.explanation: In single replacement reactions, a more reactive metal will replace a less reactive metal from its compound. This is determined by the activity series of metals.

questions.question: Double replacement: AB + CD →
questions.explanation: The general form of a double replacement reaction is AB + CD → AD + CB, where the cations and anions exchange partners.

questions.question: What speeds up reaction without being consumed?
questions.explanation: A catalyst is a substance that increases the rate of a chemical reaction without being consumed or permanently changed in the process. It works by lowering the activation energy.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You've mastered reaction types and energy changes! 🧪⚡
scoreMessages.70: Good job! Review the general forms of different reaction types.
scoreMessages.50: Satisfactory. Focus on identifying synthesis, decomposition, and replacement reactions.
scoreMessages.0: Please review reaction types, balancing equations, and energy changes in chemical reactions.
